one_line_takeaway: CRAFT shows that fine-tuning (SFT + DPO) an LLM directly on victim-ranker feedback -- rather than relying on heuristics, surrogates, or repeated live querying -- produces black-box adversarial rank-boosting content that outperforms prior sentence/trigger/document-level attacks on both promotion strength and stealth, and transfers across cross-encoder, embedding, and LLM-based rankers.
key_finding: On MS MARCO Dev Easy-5, CRAFT_Llama3.3 achieves 44.5% Top-10 and 95.8% Top-50 (statistically significant, p<0.05) with a Boost of 59.7 ranks, versus the strongest baseline EMPRA at 43.5% Top-10 / 93.4% Top-50 / 57.6 Boost. On the harder Hard-5 group, CRAFT_Llama3.3 reaches 22.2% Top-10 and 70.5% Top-50 (Boost 940.5), far exceeding the best baseline AttChain (12.2% Top-10, 42.4% Top-50, Boost 855.2). Results generalize to TREC DL 2019/2020 (e.g., Hard-5 Top-10 of 56.9%/53.8% for CRAFT_Llama3.3 vs. EMPRA's 44.2%/44.6% best baseline) and transfer across four additional victim architectures (DistilRoBERTa, ELECTRA, Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B, RankZephyr/RankLLM), with statistically significant gains on most Top-10/Top-50/Boost columns. Ablation shows DPO adds substantial gains over SFT alone (e.g., MS MARCO Hard-5 Top-10 rises from 9.2% (SFT) to 22.2% (SFT+DPO) for CRAFT_Llama3.3, a 141% relative increase), and attack performance scales monotonically with training data volume (Easy-5 ASR rises from 82% at 40% of Diamond data to 97% at 100%). CRAFT also achieves the best fidelity/stealth trade-off: perplexity stays near-original (42-45 vs. 45.1 baseline) and it has the highest adversarial-detection-pass and lowest spam-flag rates among competitive methods, while EMPRA and AttChain, though strong attackers, are flagged far more often by the OSD spam filter due to heavier lexical insertion (ATI up to 32.7 vs. CRAFT's ~19-24). A failure analysis of CRAFT_Llama3.3 (100 sampled failures each for Easy-5/Hard-5) finds Content Redundancy dominates Easy-5 failures (40/100 cases) while Topical Misalignment dominates Hard-5 failures (67/100 cases), indicating the attack's bottleneck shifts from 'no new signal to add' on easy targets to 'can't bridge the semantic gap' on hard targets.
core_contribution: The paper introduces CRAFT (Context-awaRe Adversarial Fine-Tuning of LLMs for rank attacks), a supervised, black-box adversarial attack framework against Neural Ranking Models (NRMs). Unlike prior work that relies on heuristics, gradient access, or surrogate models, CRAFT trains an LLM in three stages: (1) an adversarial dataset generation pipeline that combines retrieval-augmented generation with an iterative LLM-vs-NRM feedback loop (self-refinement) to produce validated (query, context, payload document, adversarial sentence, insertion position) tuples; (2) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) of a base LLM (DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B or QwQ-32B, yielding CRAFT_Llama3.3 and CRAFT_Qwen3) on the curated 'Diamond' dataset via maximum-likelihood estimation; and (3) Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) that contrasts successful (top-k) vs. unsuccessful adversarial sentence completions to further align generation with the rank-promotion objective. At inference time the fine-tuned model generates adversarial sentences in a single forward pass, without further queries to the victim ranker, and the sentence is inserted into the payload document to boost its rank while leaving the rest of the document unchanged.
threat_model: Black-box adversary who can only submit queries to the victim NRM and observe the resulting document ordering (not raw relevance scores), consistent with commercial search/ranking APIs. The attacker controls only the content of the target 'payload' document (e.g., a web page they own, as in SEO manipulation) and cannot alter the ranker, the rest of the corpus, or the query distribution. The victim NRM is queried only during offline training/dataset construction to obtain ranking feedback and preference pairs; once CRAFT is trained, inference requires no further access to the victim model, making the attack cheap to deploy repeatedly. The adversarial modification is constrained to appending a fluent, non-query-copying, coherence-passing sentence ('attack vector') to the payload; the original payload text must remain unaltered (content fidelity objective O1), the injected text must be fluent and hard to detect (O2), and the result must push the payload into the top-k ranks (O3, formalized as Rank(q, d_adv) <= k in Eq. 2).
limitations: The paper's own limitations/future-work section notes: (1) CRAFT currently optimizes for individual query-document pairs and does not address multi-query attacks where a single perturbation must promote a payload across a cluster of semantically related queries; (2) content fidelity (semantic similarity) is only measured at evaluation time, not enforced as an explicit constraint during dataset construction, so future Diamond-style datasets could filter on a fidelity threshold directly; (3) the failure analysis (Section 6.8) shows attack success is fundamentally limited by query difficulty -- redundancy saturation on easy targets and topical misalignment on hard targets -- rather than by any single fixable defect; (4) the study is confined to English web-search retrieval (MS MARCO / TREC DL); multilingual and domain-specific retrieval settings are left open; (5) RL-MARA, a document-level baseline from prior work, was excluded because its implementation is not public and the paper's technical details were insufficient to reproduce faithfully. The authors also candidly note that some inflated Boost numbers for Easy-5 targets under alternate victim models (Tables 6-8, exceeding 100 average rank) are partly an artifact of the target-sampling procedure (ranks 51-100 under the primary victim can fall above rank 100 under other victims), not purely attack strength.

datasets_used: MS MARCO V1 passage collection (8.8M passages, 500,000+ training queries, 6,980 dev queries)
Self-constructed Gold and Diamond adversarial datasets derived from 1,000 sampled MS MARCO Dev queries (Diamond retains 875 queries; 800 train / 200 test split)
Mixture evaluation subset: 100 query-document pairs (50 Easy-5 + 50 Hard-5) sampled from held-out MS MARCO Dev test queries
quantitative_results: MS MARCO Dev Easy-5: CRAFT_Llama3.3 Top-10=44.5%, Top-50=95.8%, Boost=59.7 vs. best baseline EMPRA Top-10=43.5%, Top-50=93.4%, Boost=57.6
MS MARCO Dev Hard-5: CRAFT_Llama3.3 Top-10=22.2%, Top-50=70.5%, Boost=940.5 vs. best baseline AttChain Top-10=12.2%, Top-50=42.4%, Boost=855.2
TREC DL 2019 Hard-5: CRAFT_Llama3.3 Top-10=56.9%, Top-50=84.1%, Boost=958.2 vs. best baseline EMPRA Top-10=44.2%, Top-50=74.4%, Boost=943.2 (28.7% relative Top-10 improvement stated by authors)
TREC DL 2020 Hard-5: CRAFT_Llama3.3 Top-10=53.8%, Top-50=81.4%, Boost=960.3 vs. best baseline EMPRA Top-10=44.6%, Top-50=71.6%, Boost=930.2
Ablation (DPO contribution), MS MARCO Hard-5, CRAFT_Llama3.3: SFT-only Top-10=9.2%/Top-50=46.8%/Boost=869.9 -> SFT+DPO Top-10=22.2%/Top-50=70.5%/Boost=940.5
Training data scaling (CRAFT_Qwen3, MS MARCO Easy-5): ASR rises from 82% (40% of Diamond data) to 97% (100%); Top-50 from 61% to 91%; Top-10 from 11% to 37%
Naturalness (Table 10): CRAFT_Llama3.3 perplexity 42.5 and Acceptability Score 0.8, matching Original documents (perplexity 45.1, AcS 0.8); Brittle-BERT AcS drops to 0.2, PRADA to 0.1
Content fidelity (MS MARCO Dev Easy-5): CRAFT SS=0.9, ATI=19.9 vs. EMPRA SS=0.9 but ATI=29.8 and AttChain ADT=38.8 (higher distortion)
Cross-model transfer to RankZephyr/RankLLM (MS MARCO Easy-5): CRAFT_Llama3.3 ASR=88.7%, Top-10=19.0%, Top-50=62.2%, Boost=37.9 vs. best baseline AttChain ASR=75.9%, Top-10=15.9%, Top-50=48.5%

Failure analysis of CRAFT_Llama3.3 (100 sampled cases each): Easy-5 failures dominated by Content Redundancy (40 cases) and Topical Misalignment (24); Hard-5 failures dominated by Topical Misalignment (67 cases)
baselines_compared: PRADA (word-level substitution via surrogate model, Wu et al.)
PAT (trigger-based via surrogate, Liu et al.)
IDEM (sentence-level, BART-generated connection sentences, Chen et al.)
EMPRA (sentence-level, embedding-perturbation-guided, Bigdeli et al. -- an author's own prior work)
AttChain (document-level, LLM chain-of-thought iterative rewriting with live querying, Liu et al.)
